citation.title: Revisiting the distinct concepts of critical flux and threshold flux

content: A B S T R A C T Classically the fouling of membranes has been categorised into four modes. Apart from the pore filling mode, the other three seek to model surface fouling be it the foundational layer on the membrane itself or the cake layer that may or may not be built up upon the initial layer. The assumptions are rigorously and critically reexamined. With more realistic assumptions, a new expression is derived for the foundational layer for both constant flux and constant pressure operation. Herein the virtues of assessing the evolution of overall resistance with time is emphasised and the usefulness of examining the evolution of resistance with respect to volume collected is illustrated with data from Membrane Bioreactors. Threshold flux is best defined with respect to rates of fouling and is the flux for a particular operation at which or below which a low and near constant rate of fouling occurs, but above which the rate of fouling increases markedly. When quoting a value for threshold flux, the length of operating is highly relevant. The definition for critical flux (strong form) remains as the flux at which the transmembrane pressure starts to deviate from the pure water line, i.e. the total resistance is identical to Rm, the resistance of the clean membrane. For the weak form, there is the assumption that following very modest rapid fouling on start-up, the flux–TMP relationship remains linear until the critical flux (weak form) is exceeded.

citation.title: Revisiting the distinct concepts of critical flux and threshold flux

content: 1. Introduction Membrane fouling is closely related to the concept of critical flux. Therefore, an examination of fouling models is necessary before we revisit the concepts of critical flux and threshold flux. Whilst originally introduced for constant pressure operation the concept of critical flux has more recently been found to be applicable to constant flux albeit under idealised conditions. Why this observation was made only rela­tively recently is a question that will be answered later. First it is observed that a JMS review paper in 2006 noted that over the previous ten years the literature had come to contain “many usages of the term “critical flux”, each constructed in order to satisfy a particular view of the author(s)” [1]. Believing that the idea had value (a view which we would endorse) they paraphrased Samuel Butler1 (1835–1902) by noting that “we must recognise that there is an idea, which is to be brought out of the wilderness and secured”. They went on to say that it is time to disentangle various concepts that have arisen around the critical flux postulate and repair the wall of its definition. Now 20 years on our aims are in part to perform a similar function and include a review of the concept of threshold flux that was introduced, in the context of fouling rates, a few years later by Field and Pearce [2]. Additionally the opportunity to revisit the fundamentals underlying Hermia’s models [3], which were adapted for crossflow filtration by

citation.title: Revisiting the distinct concepts of critical flux and threshold flux

content: Field et al. [4], has led to a more physically realistic expression for the initial foulant layer on the membrane surface together with further clarification of the critical flux concept. In contrast to Hermia’s assumption, the new expression does not assume that a layer of partic­ulate foulant completely prevents any flow through the projected area underneath the particles. The new model is labelled as a hindrance model, because the flow into the pores is hampered i.e. impeded. Section 3 includes derivations of the hindrance model for both constant flux and constant pressure operation. In advance of presenting the new work, a brief summary of the key expressions, which have been developed over the last 30+ years, is provided. At the outset a physical basis as to why there is a critical flux is revisited. In 1995, the first of two key papers to define the critical flux concept were published. Bacchin et al. [5] proposed a model that balanced surface interaction, diffusion and convection. This gave a physical explanation for the “colloid flux paradox”. Due consideration of other transport phenomena such as diffusion, shear induced diffusion and lateral migration had suggested that the flux of colloidal suspen­sions (particle size between 10 nm and 10 μm) is surprisingly high; hence the use of the word ‘paradox’. See Fig. 1.

content: Although restricted to the filtration of colloidal systems, this definition has the clear merit of being linked to a physical mechanism and the “criticality” is the point at which the repulsive barrier is overcome. Although the second paper [4] took a purely empirical approach with regard to physical mechanisms, it included a mathematical analysis for constant pressure crossflow filtration, building upon the prior work of others [3,6] and the critical flux was defined as the “flux below which a decline of flux with time does not occur; above it fouling is observed”. Subsequently Field and Wu [7] introduced a set of equations explicitly linking the removal term to shear stress. As shown in Fig. 2, two distinct forms of critical flux were envisaged. The strong form of the critical flux is the flux at which the trans­membrane pressure starts to deviate from the pure water line, i.e. up until that point the relationship between flux and transmembrane pressure is not only linear but the gradient is the same as one would have for pure water and thus equivalent to Rm, the resistance of the clean membrane. For the weak form, there is the assumption that there is very modest rapid fouling on start-up and so the flux–TMP relationship is linear but below that of the pure water line. When concluding their JMS review paper on critical and sustainable fluxes Bacchin et al.

content: relationship is linear but below that of the pure water line. When concluding their JMS review paper on critical and sustainable fluxes Bacchin et al. [1] observed that in addition to the original forms of critical flux, which were labelled as Jcs and Jcw a third usage, which was labelled as the critical flux for irreversibility Jci had arisen. Now twenty years later the third form might be beneficially linked to the concept of threshold flux in order to retain clarity of definition for the critical flux concept. They also noted that the strong form and weak form of the critical flux, Jcs and Jcw, must be evaluated via a check on whether or not the overall resis­tance has remained invariant. Herein a more general emphasis is placed upon the utility of examining resistance. The applicability of the critical flux theory or at least its ethos has been extended from microfiltration to ultrafiltration and nanofiltration. Indeed, one of us recalls that in the early 2000’s that an industrial consultant specialising in seawater desalination found the concept of a “critical” flux extremely useful. Whilst the concept of a true critical flux is only applicable when a true steady state is established, such as in a laboratory test cell operating under crossflow conditions, the concept of a threshold is crucial to understanding membrane operation in practice. For example, in the application of UF membranes in the water industry, dead-end (direct-flow) designs are used – see Fig. 3.

citation.title: Revisiting the distinct concepts of critical flux and threshold flux

content: ne operation in practice. For example, in the application of UF membranes in the water industry, dead-end (direct-flow) designs are used – see Fig. 3. However direct-flow is a pseudo steady-state operation with different fouling characteristics to crossflow, and thus the critical flux concept has limited applicability for membrane modules which operate in this manner. Field and Pearce [2] introduced the concept of a threshold flux as that flux at or below which a low and near constant rate of fouling occurs but above which the rate of fouling occurs markedly. Although introduced with direct-flow systems to the fore, the concept of threshold flux can also be applicable to crossflow systems. In general terms the threshold flux can be taken to be the flux that divides a low fouling region from a high fouling region. It is linked to the concept of a sustainable flux which is the flux at which a modest degree of fouling occurs, providing a compromise between capital expenditure (which is reduced by using higher flux) and operating costs (which are reduced by restricting the fouling rate). Whilst the threshold flux can potentially be linked to physical phenomena alone, the sustainable flux also depends upon economic factors, such as local energy costs and land values etc, and is thus of a different nature to critical and threshold fluxes. The distinction between sustainable and threshold fluxes was expounded and illustrated elsewhere [2] but will not be discussed herein. 2.

Insights from classical modelling of membrane fouling 2.1. General remarks regarding constant pressure operation It is instructive to revisit, for constant pressure operation, the deri­vation of the complete pore blocking and intermediate pore blocking models under crossflow conditions. Those interested in the details may wish to view Table A1 in the Appendix; it provides three sets of ex­pressions that have been developed at various point in the last 30 years, two of which are for constant pressure operation. The table includes mention of n values which originate with Hermia [3] and his analysis of dead-end filtration. Whilst the origins of the classical analysis dates back to the 1930’s, it is generally accepted that he was the first person to give a physical derivation of the expression for the intermediate blocking mode and to present a single unifying expression for the four modes – Equation (1). Although each mode is linked by Equation (1), it is important to note that the terms kn are dependent upon n and are rarely calculated. content: (5b) where R∞ is the asymptotic overall resistance for large time. It follows, for dead-end operation, that the term in parentheses in Equation (5a) is one. Researchers can gain insights into the fouling mechanism(s) by using Equation (5a). For example if the mechanism were to be inter­mediate blocking (or modelled as intermediate blocking) then (because n = 1) a plot of dR/dt vs R would provide a sound linear fit to the data with the intercept yielding a value of Kn and the gradient being -Kn/R∞. From our experience, it is common for this expression to adequately fit the data at early times but the trend in the data and the calculated value of R∞ are such as to often indicate a switch in fouling mechanism at later times. The use of resistance reveals a distinct difference between the four modes with dR/dt increasing with R for n > 1; continuously for dead-end filtration and initially for n = 2 as shown in Fig. 5. For n = 1 the variation of dR/dt with R is linear irrespective of the value of R∞ whilst for cake build-up (n = 0), the curves are concave up because dR/dt decreases with increasing R. These observations suggest that examination of the evolution of resistance may be particularly informative when seeking to differentiate the modes of fouling. 2.2.

content: Subsequently Ho and Zydney [9] developed a model combining pore blocking and cake filtration to represent their data on the evolution of transmembrane pressure profiles during constant flux microfiltration of BSA. Similarly, Bolton et al. [10] studied various combined models, each incorporating two fouling mechanisms, and discovered that a combined complete pore blocking and cake filtration model provided the closest conformity with the outcomes from IgG and BSA filtration experiments. However none of these models are able to describe constant flux crossflow filtration because there is no allowance for possible foulant removal by crossflow shearing forces. Moreover not only were the American Water Works Association (AWWA) able to note in 2008 that the modelling of fouling mechanisms in constant flux crossflow filtration is scarce [11] but the same was true until relatively recently. The first significant attempt to correct this omission, which is important because many industrial applications of membrane separations are operated in this manner, was by Kirschner et al. [12] whose equations are given in Table A1. The term ΣJc is, for unit area, the rate at which area is covered by the convection of foulant towards the membrane surface and B is the per unit area foulant resuspension rate (from covered areas) with units of s-1. Owing to the nature of the foulant used in their study, Kirschner et al. [12] specified the term B as particle resuspension rate. Clearly in developing this and similar equations there is an implicit assumption that the fluid mechanics of operation are able to maintain an efficient dynamic bal­ance such that the cessation of flux and the maintenance of crossflow would restore the membrane to its original clean condition. When this is not so the dynamic balance is an approximation. Now if ΣJc/B > 1.0, the model predicts that there is a finite time to complete blockage of the whole membrane that is dependent upon the ratio ΣJc/B and the term B, as described in the Appendix (section A2). Up to, and only up to, the point of blockage is the model physical realistic. However the model is physically realistic for ΣJc/B < 1.0 for all t because complete blockage is not predicted. When this ratio is greater than one complete blockage would occur after a certain time as detailed in the Appendix, section A 2. However, when the ratio is less than one the asymptote of the operating pressure (and of the corresponding relative resistance) is finite. The latter behaviour is clearly illustrated in Fig. 6(b). The asymptote, for ΣJc/B < 1.0, is [1 -ΣJcB ]-1 e.g. for ΣJc/B = 0.6 the asymptote is 2.5 as shown in Fig. 6(b). Clearly if the concentration of the foulant is low the crossflow could be sufficient to give ΣJc/B = 0.2 and an asymptote of 1.25. As an aside it is interesting to ask why until relatively recently there was no interest in the possibility highlighted by Fig. 6(b), namely that under constant flux crossflow operation one can obtain a steady state even when the fouling is that of complete pore blocking provided ΣJc/B < 1.0. To be definitive about why something has not been found previously is impossible. However as far as we can ascertain the empirical evidence was neither there nor anticipated. Fortunately the Freeman laboratory adapted the method of flux stepping for constant flux crossflow fouling studies [13] and in due course this led to the unexpected result that one could achieve steady state with R/Rm > 1 for crossflow operation under constant flux conditions [12].

The key paper [12] contains the statement that is equates to saying that ΣJc/B must “always lie between 0 and 1”. This needs qualification as it is only true if one is confining the modelling to a search for steady-state solutions. As already stated above, when the ratio ΣJc/B is less than one the asymp­tote of the operating pressure (and of the corresponding relative resis­tance, R/Rm) is finite. Furthermore, even when the ratio is equal to or greater than unity, operation could occur for a finite time up to the point of blockage (or the point when operating pressure reached a physical limit). With crossflow, the time to blockage is slower than that for dead-end filtration. As shown in the Appendix the rate can be modest particularly for low concentrations of foulants.

Complete pore blocking model When this model of complete pore blocking is adopted for membrane filtration, the assumption is that if a small area, say Δa, is covered by foulant, then all of the pores under that area Δa are completely blocked. This model strictly applies to straight through pores, so that a blocked pore delivers no flux. Intermediate pore blocking model For this mode of fouling all three papers made the same key assumption, namely that there is no bias for foulant to deposit upon the unfouled region of the membrane; the probability for foulant to deposit upon an area of clean membrane, rather than on an area previously fouled, is proportional to the instantaneous value of the area of clean membrane. Thus the rate of convection of new foulant onto unfouled membrane area is equal to aJcΣ, which contrasts with AJcΣ that is the equivalent term for complete pore blocking, n = 2. Under that mode, the incoming foulant “targets” pristine areas. Now as both modes of fouling (n = 1 and n = 2) assume that the fouled area is completely imperme­able, further deposition onto an already fouled area under the inter­mediate blocking mode of fouling (n = 1) is modelled to have no further effect.

From the assumptions underlying the conventional models, such values would be predicted when the mem­brane surface is mostly covered by particles, which can readily occur at high foulant concentrations and high permeate flux values as illustrated in Fig. 9. To tackle this issue, complete blockage is not assumed, rather the coverage is assumed to lead to hindrance of the flow as illustrated in Fig. 10. Herein the label ‘hindrance’ is preferred to ‘coverage’ because ‘hindrance’ of the flow is assumed in contrast to blockage of the flow. Additionally, the coverage of the membrane at steady state may, as demonstrated later, only be partial. In section 3, the derivation for constant flux crossflow filtration will be reprised and the concept of ‘complete coverage’ challenged. Additionally, for the first time the derivation for constant pressure filtration is developed and the results of the two compared. Meantime to complete the current sub-section on Derivations, Definitions and their Underlying Assumptions a few pre­liminary remarks will be made about the strong form of critical flux and about threshold flux. content: 2.3.3. Critical flux compared to threshold flux To infer that there is no fouling at all is a metrology challenge and one approach is to measure the rate of increase of TMP in order to establish whether or not it is essentially zero. Fig. 11 (a) from Ref. [15] illustrates this approach with respect to the microfiltration of algae (Chlorella sorokiniana). That paper also focussed upon the insights that can be gained from direct microscopic observation (DOTM) from the permeate side of the membrane surface. The membranes used in those experiments were Anopore inorganic membranes (Anodisc, Whatman) with a nominal pore size of 0.2 μm. These membranes are not only hy­drophilic but transparent when wet and this allows direct microscopic observation from the permeate side of the membrane surface. The rate of algal cell deposition observed by DOTM (expressed as the rate of membrane surface coverage) was compared to the rate of TMP rise. Findings from this study confirmed that the DOTM technique provided a more sensitive indicator of membrane fouling than the widely used TMP measurement method but it is limited not only to laboratory studies but is confined to the few membranes that are transparent when wet. The effect of shear-induced cross-flow velocity on algae deposition was clearly visualised and the results are displayed in Fig. 11(b).

The results can be viewed as suggesting a true critical flux (strong form) at low fluxes and a larger threshold flux (transition from lower fouling rate to higher fouling rate) at a higher flux, and that these values increase with increasing higher cross-flow velocities. The maximum flux at which the d(TMP)/dt remains low, is called ‘threshold’ flux instead of critical flux because this boundary marks the transition from low fouling rate to higher fouling rate rather than the transition between no fouling and fouling. 3. Hindrance model This new model does not assume that if an area, say Δa, is covered by foulant, then all of the pores under that area Δa are completely blocked. To be more precise it assumes that the flow into covered pores is impeded rather than blocked; hence the model is called the Hindrance model. It makes no prior assumptions as to the extent of the surface coverage and the extent depends upon the balance between deposition and removal assuming that the conditions for resuspension exist. If instead of a repulsive surface interaction there is an attractive one, then for the deposition of the first layer, the ‘B’ value will be zero. In the remainder of this section it is assumed that there is a finite value of ‘B’. 3.1.

However, the unfouled area presents only the membrane resistance to the flow, and as constant operating pressure is maintained, the local flux in the unfouled area is J0. Thus the complete coverage model [14] can be considered to be a special and approximated case of the hindrance model. The fractional deposition constant, Φ, was strongly influenced by the crossflow velocity. At low crossflow (<0.1 m/s, shear rate <430 s-1) Φ = 1.0, but at 0.22 m/s (shear rate = 940 s-1) Φ decreased to about 0.1. The term ‘B’ in the model will increase with the crossflow velocity. 4. Observations of fouling in MBRs and in RO relevant to the threshold flux concept 4.1. MBR fouling The TMP profile in Fig. 12(a) for the low flux case is a classic ‘TMP jump’ where there is a relatively long and low rate of change in TMP followed by a rapid rise in TMP [17]. This behaviour is typical of MBRs (see Refs. [18,19] as well as other membrane applications including RO [20,21]. The TMP jump requires a constant flux operation and a rapid transition to ‘criticality’ in the foulant layer. It is a reproducible, flux-driven phenomena. Several possible mechanisms for the TMP jump in MBRs were advanced [18,19] and these included slow fouling by macromolecular and colloidal species that causes local area loss, pore loss, or pore narrowing that raises the local fluxes above the critical for the dominant foulant, or a critical suction condition that causes cake coagulation, or loss of percolation (inner cake connectivity). Now examining resistance vs volume is very relevant and instructive (see Figs. 12(c) and 13(b).

content: ion, or loss of percolation (inner cake connectivity). Now examining resistance vs volume is very relevant and instructive (see Figs. 12(c) and 13(b). However particularly for submerged MBRs under suction, where the maximum TMP is constrained, the dependency that TMP has upon other variables is of practical importance; hence the inclusion of Fig. 12(d) illustrating the relationship between the increase in TMP and accumulation of permeate for various set fluxes. In com­menting upon the data used in Fig. 12 it was observed [22] that at the end of the low flux run – the one with the TMP jump – the Confocal Laser Scanning Microscopy had showed a high EPS content next to the membrane surface with a maximum fraction of EPS in excess of 0.4 which is double that for the other runs [17]. Field et al. [22] suggested that the high rate of increase in TMP after day 20 was due to the greater number of dead cells. Guglielmi et al. [23], whose data is in Fig. 13, also attributed the transition to bound and free forms of EPS. It has also been shown that sludge retention time (SRT) is an important factor in determining the time of operation before a TMP jump [19]. As longer

content: SRTs can lead to lower excess sludge production owing to dead and inactive microorganisms being consumed by other microbes, this finding corroborates the hypothesis that TMP jumps are caused by cell lysis. In reflecting on the fluid flow through a cake being clogged by cell debris, it is pertinent to note that the specific cake resistance is highly dependent upon porosity. For instance, the formula for the CarmanKozeny equation relates the specific cake resistance (α) with porosity (ε) as follows: α∝(1 -ε)2/ϵ3. Taking just one example it is readily computed that a change from a porosity of 0.3 to one of 0.2 would change the value of specific cake resistance by a factor of 4.4. 4.2. Fouling in RO Through combing electrical impedance spectrometry (EIS) and TMP measurements Ho et al. [21] showed that for their specific operating conditions generating biofouling, the TMP jump was due to a critical compositional change in the fouling layer. The interpretation made was that this was the point at which a biofilm starts to dominate; specifically extracellular polymeric substances (EPS) fill a matrix of bacteria. Such an observation is in accord with the comment made at the end of section 4.1. However as noted in the next section, some results originally inter­preted by us and others as being linked to a threshold flux are better understood as being linked to a critical flux. 5. Reflections 5.1. General remarks The terms critical flux and threshold flux have sometimes been confused.

content: stood as being linked to a critical flux. 5. Reflections 5.1. General remarks The terms critical flux and threshold flux have sometimes been confused. For example, Ho et al. [21] used non-invasive electrical impedance spectrometry (EIS) to measure the conditions in the polar­isation layer of RO membranes experiencing colloidal fouling at con­stant flux. The conductance of the polarized layer had been identified as the main EIS parameter associated with membrane fouling. Conduc­tance rises as salt concentration rises and falls as colloid concentration rises (occupies space and reduces conducting pathway). EIS was measured along with TMP for flux steps made every 2 h from 15 to 40 LMH. A distinct transition was observed for both TMP and EIS conduc­tance. The gradient of the TMP measurements showed dTMP/dt rising from essentially zero to increasing values as flux increased and conductance showed a distinct minimum with flux; both transitions were at the same flux. The EIS data are consistent with transition from a flowing colloidal suspension polarized layer to a stagnant cake layer with growing cake enhanced concentration polarisation of salt solution of higher conductance. It should be noted that Ho et al. [21] interpreted the transition as threshold flux but re-evaluation of the data suggests that the data show Jcrit behaviour for RO and colloids.

Although the emphasis was upon determining the rate of fouling through examination of dTMP/dt, the transition was from essential zero fouling to fouling. Thus depending upon the value of the overall resistance, the transition point corresponded to either the strong form of the critical flux, Jcs, or to the weak form of the critical flux, Jcw. The term subcritical flux operation was originally coined by Howell [24] who discussed the complexities of mixtures in which different components have different critical fluxes. Sometimes cleaning is easier if one operates above the critical flux of one of the components that acts as a filter aid screening out a finer component with the ‘filter-aid’ component being something that is also readily removed. Nominally sub-critical operation discussed by Hughes and Field [25] but subse­quently the concept of a threshold flux has emerged and is discussed fully in section 5.2. Emphasis has just been placed upon the word ‘nominally’ because titles such as “Sub-critical fouling in a membrane bioreactor ...” are oxymoronic if sub-critical is deemed to refer to operation below a critical flux for fouling because fouling has already

[26] showed in that if a critical flux were to exist for MBRs then its value would be very low and certainly less than 5 L/m2/h. Now such low values are not used in practice and operation is certainly not sub-critical with respect to the strong form or weak form of the critical flux, Jcs or Jcw. 5.2. Threshold flux Field and Pearce [2] have provided a firm basis and definition of Jth. Three key criteria from that paper are selected and then a refinement will be suggested after considering some of the MBR data presented earlier. 1. “The threshold flux is that flux at which or below which a low and near constant rate of fouling occurs, but above which the rate of fouling increases markedly”;2. Focus is “on determining the threshold flux for a particular opera­ tion”; and3. “If there is a break point in the rate of fouling vs flux curve a threshold flux can be determined”. Now consider Fig. 12(a). For operating times of 10, 15, 20 days the low flux (13 L/m2/h) data indicates low fouling, whereas the middle and high fluxes show significant fouling rates as confirmed by Fig. 12(c). The apparent Jth is about 13 LMH using an operating period up to 20 days. This is a relatively long period of constant operation. However if the operation is allowed to continue for another 5 days the fouling rate (dTMP/dt) for low flux is also significant.

This would change the determination of Jth if the ‘particular operation’ involved an operation period of ≥25 days. In other words the observed Jth is limited to a specific period, and this should be stated. Operation beyond the specific period may lead to unstainable TMP rise, or the ‘TMP jump’. The specific period is 20d for the data in Fig. 12 and around 18 days for the data in Fig. 13. All of the data in Fig. 13 displays TMP jumps with Fig. 13(b) suggests that an intermediate flux generates the superior performance. 5.3. Hurdles to development of successful membrane processes A brief summary of our reflections on the distinct concepts of critical flux and threshold flux are given in Table 1. It is also apposite to briefly examine their place in the development of a successful membrane pro­cess. As noted by Beuscher et al. [27] in their relatively recent paper titled “Membrane research beyond materials science” one can view the development of a successful process that has membranes at its core as having cleared four hurdles which will be listed as a sequence but “the hurdles ... require continuous iteration, backward as well as forward” [27]. The hurdles are (i) material to membrane; (ii) membrane to module; (iii) membrane module to membrane process; and (iv) integration of membrane process into an overall process.

citation.title: Revisiting the distinct concepts of critical flux and threshold flux

content: ial to membrane; (ii) membrane to module; (iii) membrane module to membrane process; and (iv) integration of membrane process into an overall process. As they noted when discussing the third hurdle, research on the development of a membrane process often comprises the addressing of just one central issue: “how can the driving force required for the separation be opti­mized and leveraged sustainably?” [27]. The main tools for process design are multi-stage configurations and the selection of operating conditions. The range of options available is such that this stage offers scope for optimisation. To reduce the size of the design space that they need to consider, engineers designing membrane processes have generally put their faith in a few heuristic rules. One such rule was that to decrease membrane area (and thus capital cost) membrane flux should be increased by increasing the driving force. As Beuscher et al. [27] noted this rule was faithfully followed in microfiltration and ultrafiltration until the advent of the critical flux theory and they cited Field et al. [4]. Their concluding summary was that “by operating below the so-called critical flux, per­formance can be sustained on longer time scales, leading to long-term stable operation at a flux that is higher than the average flux that would have been obtained if the initial driving force had been higher. This is a great example of academic research with significant commer­cial impact” [27].

Remarks on the constraints of module design and operation are also pertinent because the appropriate operating flux is module dependent. As explained in section 1, critical flux is linked to the balance between convection towards and back transport away from the membrane. The same is true of the degree of concentration polarisation. Now trium­phant articles suggesting that ‘ultrapermeable membranes’ (UPMs) including those incorporating aquaporins or carbon nanotubes or gra­phene materials will usher in a new era of cheap desalination generally overlook the limitations caused by concentration polarisation. Any quest to exploit improved membranes of higher permeance will be constrained by the ability (or inability) to control concentration polarisation [28]. This is particularly important with regard to RO as discussed elsewhere. As illustrated in section 2.2 of [28] there is the need for improved modules to match higher permeability RO membranes. As demonstrated such modules would need to have significantly higher mass transfer coefficients (ca 10x). This suggests that progress at the R&D stage will be best achieved if some thought is given to the boundary layer Peclet number which is defined as flux divided by boundary layer mass transfer coefficient [29].

content: A 2.1. Physical interpretation for ΣJc/B < 1 Take the example of ΣJc/B = 0.6, where the asymptote value of R/Rmin is 2.5. The physical interpretation is that at any instance close to the asymptote 60 % of the surface is blocked and 40 % is open to filtration. However there is a dynamic balance between uncovering and covering. As the overall volumetric flowrate has been maintained at ‘constant flux operation’ the fixed flowrate is now passing through 40 % of the filter area, hence the two-and-a-half-fold increase in operating pressure and relative resistance. If the overall area of filter is A m2 then the amount of foulant arriving in a time interval of Δt covers an area of ΣJcAΔt (because the incoming mass is JAΔt.c and area covered per unit mass is Σ). Given the assumptions made for R.W. Field et al. Journal of Membrane Science 741 (2026) 124961 n = 2, which include that the incoming foulant falls upon uncovered membrane area, this value remains unchanged. Now removal (uncovering of area) is only from the covered (fouled) area, af, i.e. from (A -a). Thus once steady state has been achieved ΣJcAΔt = BafΔt i.e. ac/ A = ΣJc / B. So for a ratio of 0.6, the covered area is 60 % of the overall area and this cross-checks with the fact that 40 % is open. A 2.2.

content: Also as shown in Figure A2 (b), for J0cΣB = 0.5 the fraction coverage is tending towards 0.5 for large f but is close to 0.33 for very low f. This figure suggests that for constant flux filtration the dependency of area covered at steady state depends upon the value of the hindrance factor f and the parameter ΣJ0cB . Also a discussion of Fig. A2(a), see section 3.1 in the main text.Now as already noted the rate of change of unfouled area can be expressed in one of two paradigmatic ways. For relatively low values of f one might well assume that the foulants deposit in an unbiased fashion (as with the classic n = 1 model). This leads to the results displayed by the dashed lines in Figure A2 (b) which result from the calculation based upon the above analysis. However taking an approach equivalent to that in section 3.1 solutions based upon the asymptotic steady-state mass balance of foulant generates the results displayed by the solid lines which are labelled C 0.75, C 0.5, C 0.3 and C 0.2. It is only for low value of the ratio SR that there is agreement between the two approaches. Now to fully understand the significance of these ratios to the development of the overall resistance, the net accumulation of foulant across the whole membrane would also need to be calculated to establish whether there is a steady state in foulant accumulation, or whether there is a transition to cake filtration. Detailed development is outside the scope of this work.
